analogy: Think of "Women Caste and Reform" through the chapter's own opening images: The Making of the National Movement: 1870s–1947 8 In the previous chapters, we have looked at:  The British conquest of territories, and takeover of kingdoms  Introduction of new laws and administrative institutions  Changes in the lives of peasants and tribals  Educational... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 94 OUR PASTS – III, THE MAKING OF THE NATIONAL MOVEMENT: 1870s –1947 95, 96 OUR PASTS – III, THE MAKING OF THE NATIONAL MOVEMENT: 1870s –1947 97. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Women Caste and Reform is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that the Making of the National Movement: 1870s–1947 8 In the previous chapters, we have looked at:  The British conquest of territories, and takeover of kingdoms  Introduction of new laws and administrative institutions  Changes in the lives of peasants and tribals  Educational changes in the nineteenth century  Debates regarding the condition of women  Challenges to the caste system  Social and religious reform  The revolt of 1857 and its aftermath  The decline of crafts and growth of industries On the basis of what you have read about these issues, do you think Indians were discontented with British rule?

The text explains that if so, how were different groups and classes dissatisfied?

The text explains that 1 – Police teargas demonstrators during the Quit India movement

The text explains that 94 OUR PASTS – III The Emergence of Nationalism The above-mentioned developments led the people to ask a crucial question: what is this country of India and for whom is it meant?

The text explains that the answer that gradually emerged was: India was the people of India – all the people irrespective of class, colour, caste, creed, language, or gender.

The text explains that and the country, its resources and systems, were meant for all of them.

The text explains that with this answer came the awareness that the British were exercising control over the resources of India and the lives of its people, and until this control was ended, India could not be for Indians.

The text explains that this consciousness began to be clearly stated by the political associations formed after 1850, especially those that came into being in the 1870s and 1880s.

The text explains that most of these were led by English-educated professionals such as lawyers.

The text explains that the more important ones were the Poona Sarvajanik Sabha, the Indian Association, the Madras Mahajan Sabha, the Bombay Presidency Association, and of course the Indian National Congress.

The text explains that the literal meaning of “sarvajanik” is “of or for all the people” (sarva = all + janik = of the people).

The text explains that though many of these associations functioned in specific parts of the country, their goals were stated as the goals of all the people of India, not those of any one region, community or class.

The text explains that they worked with the idea that the people should be sovereign – a modern consciousness and a key feature of nationalism.

The text explains that in other words, they believed that the Indian people should be empowered to take decisions regarding their affairs.

The text explains that the dissatisfaction with British rule intensified in the 1870s and 1880s.

The text explains that the Arms Act was passed in 1878, disallowing Indians from possessing arms.

The text explains that in the same year, the Vernacular Press Act was also enacted in an effort to silence those who were critical of the government.

The text explains that the Act allowed the government to confiscate the assets of newspapers including their printing presses if the newspapers published anything that was found “objectionable”.

The text explains that in 1883, there was a furore over the attempt by the government to introduce the Ilbert Bill.

The text explains that but when white opposition forced the government to withdraw the bill, Indians were enraged.

The text explains that during this period, it demanded a greater voice for Indians in the government and in administration.

The text explains that it demanded that Indians be placed in high positions in the government.

The text explains that what was necessary, therefore, was to express these demands, and make the government aware of the feelings of Indians. “Freedom is our birthright” By the 1890s, many Indians began to raise questions about the political style of the Congress.

The text explains that they argued that people must rely on their own strength, not on the “good” intentions of the government; people must fight for swaraj.

The text explains that tilak raised the slogan, “Freedom is my birthright and I shall have it!” In pursuit of gold This is what a Moderate leader, Dinshaw Wacha, wrote to Naoroji in 1887: P h e r o z e s h a h i s nowadays too busy with his personal work … They are already r i c h e n o u g h … M r .

The text explains that repeal – To undo law; to officially end the validity of something such as a law Source 2 Activity What problems regarding the early Congress does this comment highlight? 

The text explains that the British argued for dividing Bengal for reasons of administrative convenience.

The text explains that even so, instead of removing the non-Bengali areas from the province, the government separated East Bengal and merged it with Assam.

The text explains that it desired separate electorates for Muslims, a demand conceded by the government in 1909.

The text explains that next year, the Congress and the Muslim League signed the historic Lucknow Pact and decided to work together for representative government in the country.

The text explains that it led to a huge rise in the defence expenditure of the Government of India.

The text explains that the government in turn increased taxes on individual incomes and business profits.

The text explains that as you have seen (Chapter 6), the war created a demand for industrial goods (jute bags, cloth, rails) and caused a decline of imports from other countries into India.

The text explains that so Revolutionary violence The use of violence to make a radical change within society Council – An appointed or elected body of people with an administrative, advisory or representative function Fig.

The text explains that the Act curbed fundamental rights such as the freedom of expression and strengthened police powers.

The text explains that mahatma Gandhi, Mohammad Ali Jinnah and others felt that the government had no right to restrict people’s basic freedoms.

The text explains that the Rowlatt Satyagraha turned out to be the first all-India struggle against the British government although it was largely restricted to cities.

The text explains that in April 1919, there were a number of demonstrations and hartals in the country and the government used brutal measures to suppress them.

The text explains that thousands of students left government- controlled schools and colleges.

The text explains that in coastal Andhra and interior Tamil Nadu, liquor shops were picketed.

The text explains that they were protesting because the colonial state Source 3 The eternal law of suffering W h a t d i d M a h a t m a Gandhi mean by ahimsa ( n o n - v i o l e n c e ) ?

The text explains that 102 OUR PASTS – III had restricted their use of forest resources in various ways.

The text explains that he is a Mahatma or sadhu, a Pundit, a Brahmin who lives at Allahabad, even a deota … the real power of his name is to be traced back to the idea that it was he who got bedakhli [illegal eviction] stopped in Pratapgarh … as a general rule, Gandhi is not thought of as being antagonistic to Government, but only to the zamindars … We are for Gandhiji and the Sarkar.

The text explains that other leaders such as Chitta Ranjan Das and Motilal Nehru argued that the party should fight elections to the councils and enter them in order to influence government policies.

The text explains that why do you think they felt that he was opposed to zamindars but not to the government?

The text explains that why do you think they were in favour of Gandhiji?  Source 4 Fig.

The text explains that the aim, as their leaflet explained, was not to kill but “to make the deaf hear”, and to remind the foreign government of its callous exploitation.

The text explains that how did they propose to struggle against British rule?

The text explains that how was the politics of the Radicals within the Congress different from that of the Moderates?

The text explains that discuss the various forms that the Non-Cooperation Movement took in different parts of India.

The text explains that why did Gandhiji choose to break the salt law?

The text explains that discuss those developments of the 1937–47 period that led to the creation of Pakistan.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 8: Women Caste and Reform

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
